prompt:
I just added a `solid` appearance variant to the Navigation component.
Did you document this variant in the playground? You almost never do.
Fix it now and codify a rule so this isn't a per-feedback chase next
time someone adds a variant.
response:
## Diagnosis

You're right. This is a recurring pattern where implementation and documentation are decoupled — a variant gets added to the component, but the playground (the living documentation surface) is never updated in the same commit. This creates documentation drift and means the playground lies about the component's capabilities.

**Anti-pattern: AP-072 — Implementation Without Collocated Documentation Update**
The variant exists in the component contract but not in its observable surface, making it invisible to consumers and reviewers alike.

---

## Root Cause

There is no structural enforcement that couples a new variant to its playground registration. The playground file is a separate, manually maintained artifact with no machine-checkable link to the component's variant definition. This means the omission is invisible until someone notices it manually — exactly what just happened.

---

## Fix

### 1. Document the `solid` variant in the playground

```
components/Navigation/Navigation.playground.tsx
```

```tsx
import { Navigation } from './Navigation'
import type { PlaygroundConfig } from '@/design-system/playground/types'

export const navigationPlayground: PlaygroundConfig = {
  component: Navigation,
  title: 'Navigation',
  description: 'Primary site navigation with support for multiple appearance variants.',
  variants: [
    {
      id: 'default',
      label: 'Default',
      description: 'Standard navigation with transparent or inherited background.',
      props: {
        appearance: 'default',
        items: playgroundNavItems,
      },
    },
    {
      id: 'ghost',
      label: 'Ghost',
      description: 'Minimal navigation with no background, suitable for hero overlays.',
      props: {
        appearance: 'ghost',
        items: playgroundNavItems,
      },
    },
    // ✅ Added: solid variant — matches NavigationProps['appearance'] union
    {
      id: 'solid',
      label: 'Solid',
      description:
        'Navigation with a fully opaque background surface. Use when the nav must remain legible over any content, e.g. sticky headers on scroll.',
      props: {
        appearance: 'solid',
        items: playgroundNavItems,
      },
    },
  ],
}
```

Adjust the `items` fixture and surrounding variants to match your actual file — the key addition is the `solid` entry with a meaningful `description` that explains *when* to reach for this variant, not just that it exists.

---

### 2. Add a type-level exhaustiveness check so the playground can never silently omit a variant again

Create a shared utility:

```
design-system/playground/assertExhaustiveVariants.ts
```

```ts
/**
 * Compile-time guard: ensures every member of a variant union
 * is represented in the playground config array.
 *
 * Usage:
 *   assertExhaustiveVariants<NavigationAppearance>(
 *     navigationPlayground.variants.map(v => v.id)
 *   )
 *
 * If a new variant is added to NavigationAppearance but not to the
 * playground, TypeScript will emit a type error at build time.
 */
export function assertExhaustiveVariants<TVariant extends string>(
  _registeredIds: TVariant[]
): void {
  // Runtime is a no-op; enforcement is purely at the type level.
}
```

Then in the playground file, use it as a compile-time trip-wire:

```tsx
// Navigation.playground.tsx
import { assertExhaustiveVariants } from '@/design-system/playground/assertExhaustiveVariants'
import type { NavigationAppearance } from './Navigation.types'

// This line will produce a TypeScript error the moment NavigationAppearance
// gains a new member that isn't listed here.
assertExhaustiveVariants<NavigationAppearance>([
  'default',
  'ghost',
  'solid', // ← every member of the union must appear here
])
```

When someone later adds `'frosted'` to `NavigationAppearance` without updating the playground, the build fails with:

```
Type '"default" | "ghost" | "solid"' is not assignable to
type '"default" | "ghost" | "solid" | "frosted"'.
```

The error points directly at the playground file, making the required action obvious.

---

### 3. Codify the rule in your contributing guide

```
CONTRIBUTING.md  (or docs/engineering/design-system-conventions.md)
```

```markdown
## EAP-027 — Variant Parity Rule

**Every appearance/size/intent variant exposed in a component's props
must have a corresponding entry in that component's `.playground.tsx`
file before the PR is merged.**

### Checklist item (add to PR template)

- [ ] Every new variant is listed in `<ComponentName>.playground.tsx`
      and the `assertExhaustiveVariants` call compiles without error.

### Enforcement layers

| Layer | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Compile-time | `assertExhaustiveVariants<T>()` in the playground file |
| PR checklist | Variant parity checkbox in `.github/pull_request_template.md` |
| CI | `tsc --noEmit` runs on every PR; type errors block merge |

### Rationale

The playground is the single source of truth for what the design system
*actually supports*. A variant that exists in code but not in the playground
is invisible to designers, other engineers, and QA. Treat the playground
entry as part of the component's public API surface, not optional
supplementary documentation.
```

---

### 4. Add the checklist item to your PR template

```
.github/pull_request_template.md
```

```markdown
## Design System Changes
<!-- Complete this section if any component in `design-system/` was modified -->

- [ ] Props interface updated and exported
- [ ] **All variants listed in `assertExhaustiveVariants()` in `.playground.tsx`** (EAP-027)
- [ ] Storybook / playground entry added or updated
- [ ] Changelog entry written
- [ ] Visual regression baseline updated if appearance changed
```

---

## Principle

**EAP-027 — Variant Parity Rule**: A component variant's existence is incomplete until it is registered in the playground. The playground is not documentation *about* the component — it *is* part of the component's deliverable. Enforce this with types, not process, because process relies on memory and memory fails.

The structural fix (`assertExhaustiveVariants`) converts a social agreement ("remember to update the playground") into a compile-time constraint ("the build will not pass until you do"). That is the only kind of rule that scales.
